pi_items.verified_value: June 15 (day of year 166 in 2025, 6 days before summer solstice)

pi_items.source: Calculated using formula: LAN = 12:00 UTC + (longitude/15°/hour) + equation of time. For -10° longitude: LAN = 12:00 + 40 min = 12:40 UTC
Longitude correction: 10° W / 15°/hour = 40 minutes, equation of time ≈ 0 on June 15, so 12:00 + 40 min = 12:40 UTC

Calculated using Spencer formula: δ = 23.44° × sin((2π/365) × (day - 81)). For day 166: δ = 23.30°. Verified against references indicating sun reaches maximum declination of 23.44° on summer solstice (June 21), so June 15 value of 23.3° is correct.
Task specifies StartTime should be 20 minutes before calculated LAN (12:40 UTC), therefore 12:20 UTC
Web search confirms June 15 is very close to the zero-crossings of equation of time. Multiple sources indicate equation of time ≈ -10 seconds on June 15, essentially zero for navigation purposes.
Multiple astronomical sources confirm sun reaches maximum northern declination of 23.44° at summer solstice, occurring around June 21 each year. This is equivalent to Earth's axial tilt.
Specified in task.json metadata as reasonable tolerance for LAN time calculation
Specified in task.json metadata as reasonable tolerance for meridian altitude
Install script clones from https://github.com/bridgecommand/bc.git. Documented as a navigation/bridge simulator.

privileged_info_summary: The task involves celestial navigation calculations for a vessel at 45°N, 10°W on June 15, 2025. All astronomical ground truth values are verified: (1) The sun's declination on June 15 is 23.3°, calculated 6 days before the summer solstice when maximum declination reaches 23.44°; (2) Local Apparent Noon (LAN) occurs at 12:40 UTC, calculated as 12:00 UTC plus 40 minutes correction for 10°W longitude, with equation of time approximately zero; (3) Meridian altitude is 68.3°, calculated as 90° minus the difference between observer latitude (45°) and declination (23.3°); (4) Start time should be set 20 minutes before LAN (12:20 UTC) as instructed; (5) Tolerances of ±5 minutes for time and ±0.5° for altitude are reasonable for this exercise. No external datasets are used—this is a synthetic scenario using standard nautical calculations.
